answers.answer: B) Use the TypeScript compiler API or a language server (LSP) - it understands definitions vs. call sites, can check argument counts, and won't match string references. Reason: LSP/TypeScript API provides semantic understanding that grep/ripgrep cannot. It can: (1) distinguish function definitions from call sites from string literals; (2) validate argument counts at each call site; (3) find all references to both old and new names via symbol resolution. Option A (grep) matches strings incorrectly. Option C (ast-grep) is good but doesn't have full type checking. Option D is complex and error-prone. For a TypeScript codebase, using the TypeScript language server is the most accurate approach.

GIT WORKFLOW - STEP BY STEP:

=== STEP 1: Squash bug+fix pairs (commits 3+4, 5+6) ===

# Start interactive rebase from before commit 3
git rebase -i HEAD~8

# In the editor, change:
# pick <commit3> bug intro
# pick <commit4> bug fix
# pick <commit5> another bug
# pick <commit6> another fix

# To:
# pick <commit3> bug intro
# fixup <commit4> bug fix
# pick <commit5> another bug
# fixup <commit6> another fix

# Save and close. This squashes 3+4 and 5+6 into single commits.

# If something goes wrong:
# git rebase --abort  # Cancel rebase
# git reflog  # Find previous HEAD if needed

=== STEP 2: Rebase onto latest main ===

# Fetch latest main
git fetch origin main

# Rebase onto main
git rebase origin/main

# If conflict occurs (expected in src/auth/middleware.ts):
# Git will pause and show conflict markers

=== STEP 3: Resolve conflict in src/auth/middleware.ts ===

# Open the file, you'll see:
# <<<<<<< HEAD
# (your auth logic changes)
# =======
# (main's import structure changes)
# >>>>>>> origin/main

# Resolution: Keep BOTH - main's imports AND your auth logic
# Example resolved file:

// NEW imports from main
import { JwtService, TokenValidator } from '@auth/core';
import { Logger } from '@utils/logging';

// YOUR auth logic changes
export async function authenticate(req, res, next) {
  const token = extractToken(req);
  const validator = new TokenValidator();
  // ... your logic
}

# After editing:
git add src/auth/middleware.ts
git rebase --continue

# If rebase continues with more conflicts, repeat. If something goes wrong:
git rebase --abort  # Abort and try again

=== STEP 4: Cherry-pick commit from feature/logging ===

# Cherry-pick the specific commit
git cherry-pick abc1234

# If conflict:
# Fix conflicts, then:
git add <resolved-files>
git cherry-pick --continue

# If cherry-pick fails badly:
git cherry-pick --abort

=== STEP 5: Push and create PR ===

# Force push (history was rewritten by rebase)
git push --force-with-lease origin feature/auth-v2

# Create PR with GitHub CLI (or use web UI)
gh pr create \
  --base main \
  --head feature/auth-v2 \
  --title "feat: Auth v2 (replaces #123)" \
  --body "This PR replaces the previous auth v2 PR with cleaned up history. Commits squashed, rebased on latest main with security patch." \
  --replace-pushed-by

# Or if replacing a specific PR:
gh pr edit <old-pr-number> --set-branch feature/auth-v2

# If push fails:
# git push --force-with-lease origin feature/auth-v2
# (--force-with-lease is safer than --force, won't overwrite others' work)

=== SUMMARY OF KEY COMMANDS ===

git rebase -i HEAD~8          # Interactive rebase to squash
git fetch origin main         # Get latest main
git rebase origin/main        # Rebase onto main
git add <file>                # Stage resolved conflicts
git rebase --continue         # Continue after resolving
git cherry-pick abc1234       # Apply specific commit
git push --force-with-lease   # Safe force push after rebase

=== RECOVERY COMMANDS ===

git rebase --abort            # Abort rebase
git cherry-pick --abort       # Abort cherry-pick
git reflog                    # Find lost commits
git reset --hard HEAD@{n}     # Reset to previous state
